sections.text:
13.1 Process Overview
MRP consists of the following steps, as shown in Figure 13.1:
1 Check the planning file to avoid using planning materials that aren’t needed.
2 Determine the material shortage quantity (or surplus quantity) by carrying out a net
requirements calculation.
3 Calculate the procurement quantity to include lot sizes for planned orders or pur-
chase requisitions.
4 Schedule basic dates or lead times (for in-house production only).
5 Select the source of supply by determining the procurement proposal.
6 Determine the requirement for subordinate parts on the basis of the BOM explosion
by carrying out a dependent requirements determination.

13.1 Process Overview
During an SAP S/4HANA system implementation project—or even later on, when your
company wants to start taking advantage of planning tools such as MRP—you should
follow a step-by-step approach to identifying materials to plan with MRP. You can plan
just about any kind of material with MRP. Broadly speaking, as a planner, you should
consider the following factors and take the following steps:
1. To begin with, prepare a comprehensive list of all materials in a Microsoft Excel file.
This Excel file may be a template that the SAP S/4HANA system consultant will make
available. Materials can be finished goods, components, assemblies, raw materials,
packing materials, consumables, or maintenance spares. You can also plan materials
with special procurement types, such as subcontracting materials, phantom assem-
blies, pipeline materials, procurement from another plant, production in another
plant, stock transport orders (for interplant transfer), and so on.
2. Separate the materials that you want to plan from those that aren’t worth your time
and effort or the cost involved in planning. Then, delete the latter from the Excel file.
3. Once you’ve got the materials that you want to include in planning, separate them
further by classifying them into A, B, and C types. Type-A materials are high-value
ones and generally include finished goods, semifinished goods or assemblies, raw
materials, and packaging materials. Type-B and type-C materials are medium- to
low-value materials, such as consumables and routine maintenance spares.
Using deterministic replenishment procedures for all type-A materials, you individually
evaluate each and every important factor involved in MRP to see how these fit into plan-
ning to provide the desired results. For example, as a planner, you should ask the follow-
ing questions:
 What should the safety stock of this material be?
 How many days in the planning window are needed so that the system doesn’t make
any automatic changes to the production (or procurement) plan?
 When there is a requirement for a material, how much should the system plan (exact
quantity, minimum quantity, maximum quantity, etc.)?
 How should the incoming sales orders consume the planning figures?
 Is this material MTS or MTO?
 Is there a need to produce this material for a specific project or customer (via MTO)?
 Is this assembly produced in house or at another plant of the company?
 How much scrap (as a percentage) does the production of this material generate for
a product or subassembly, and therefore, can the scrap be incorporated into plan-
ning?
 Does it make sense to first forecast material requirements by using a forecasting
tool and then adjusting the forecast values before proceeding with material plan-
ning?

13 Material Requirements Planning
674
You need to ask these (and many more) questions for each material and correspond-
ingly enter or update the information in the Excel file.
For raw materials or packaging materials, which are externally procured, you need to
get answers to questions such as the following:
 What is the planned delivery time of this material?
 What is the GR processing time?
 How much underdelivery and overdelivery of a material is allowed?
 Is there a scheduling agreement or a contract (for quantity or value of a material)
with a supplier?
 Is there a quota arrangement in place if there are multiple vendors of the same
material?
We suggest involving procurement personnel from MM to help with answering these
(and many more) questions because they have firsthand knowledge of and experience
with procurement.
For type-B and type-C materials, you should consider using consumption-based plan-
ning or stochastic replenishment procedures. In consumption-based planning, you
choose whether to use reorder-based planning, which involves manually or automati-
cally suggesting the reorder point of each material based on historical consumption.
For manual reorder point planning, you enter the reorder point quantity, which may
be based on past experience or consumption figures available from another planning
program (not the SAP system). Furthermore, you can take advantage of the forecasting
tool to help you plan future consumption. Forecasting also uses historical consump-
tion data that’s available in the system.
Because planning is a continuously evolving and ongoing process, we recommend that
you continue fine-tuning the planning parameters of materials to help the system
come up with planning results that can be used in the production and procurement
processes almost immediately (and without need for significant adjustments). For
example, say that when you first entered the planned delivery time of a material, it was
five days, but as the system built up a significant database of planned delivery times in
the past year, the average delivery time turned out to be seven days. You must update
this information in the MRP view of the material master so that the future planning
results of MRP are more reflective of this reality. The system also makes available a tool
(Transaction WPDTC) to provide a comparison between the information maintained in
the material master and the actual planned delivery time that the system records for
each delivery. Several such analysis reports and tools are available in the SAP S/4HANA
system.
Whether you’re using MRP or consumption-based planning—that is, deterministic or
stochastic replenishment procedures, respectively—a large number of factors are
defined in the four different MRP views of the material master to influence the plan-
© 

675
13.1 Process Overview
ning results of MRP. In fact, the system refers to this detailed information in the MRP
views to come up with procurement proposals. Some of these factors may be how the
system performs the net requirements calculations, what percentage of safety stock
you should consider in planning, the lot-sizing procedures to use, whether forecasting
is a necessary prerequisite to planning, and how to manage special procurement types.
In the following sections, we’ll cover the important factors and parameters you’ll need
to set up to achieve the desired planning results from MRP, which you can subse-
quently use in production and procurement activities.
Figure 13.2 provides an overview of the role of MRP in the planning and production
areas of the SAP system.
Figure 13.2 Production Planning
The planning process starts with requirements from SAP S/4HANA Sales; from the
next-generation cloud-based SAP HANA tool known as SAP Integrated Business Plan-
ning for Supply Chain (SAP IBP); and from the S&OP planning process as a forecast,
which eventually becomes a part of demand management. Companies can also use
master production scheduling for planning finished products, important semifinished
products, and products that will use bottleneck production lines. After the master pro-
duction scheduling planning run, you can use the capacity leveling functionality in

capacity requirements planning to ensure that the plan proposed by master produc-
tion scheduling will not exceed the capacity of critical resources. An alternative to mas-
ter production scheduling is to use the PP/DS tool, which is now embedded into the
SAP S/4HANA digital core. PP/DS supports advanced planning features that are not
available in master production scheduling or MRP, such as planning with finite capac-
ity (i.e., considering capacity restrictions on the work centers), planning considering
batch shelf life, planning with complex heuristics, and even using the PP/DS optimizer
tool to make a feasible plan based on cost considerations.
The system also takes customer requirements into account and reflects all demand
from demand management as PIRs. A PIR with an active version consists of quantities
of a material for which a planner wants to run MRP. The system runs MRP based on the
details given in the demand program, and the outcome of the MRP run is the creation
of procurement proposals. These procurement proposals can be planned orders (order
proposals), purchase requisitions for external procurement, or delivery schedules for
scheduling agreements with vendors. Procurement proposals are divided into two sep-
arate yet interlinked areas: procurement for externally procured materials and produc-
tion for in-house produced materials.

subareas: deterministic and stochastic replenishment procedures.

Tips and Tricks
Transactions MC40 and MC41 are quite helpful for letting you as a materials planner
separate materials into A, B, and C types based on their usage or consumption values
(from the past) or requirement values (in the future). For example, you can use ABC
analysis to classify those customers that generate 75% of sales volume as A customers,
the next 15% as B customers, and the rest as C customers. Similarly, you can classify
materials by aggregating their consumption values (or sales volumes) of materials
until they reach 80% (or whatever percentage you define) of the total value and then
designate all those materials as type-A items.
You can then incorporate these ABC analyses into the ABC Indicator field in the MRP1
view of the material master. Note the A, B, and C indicators that can be made part of
their respective material masters in Figure 13.4.
You access the first screen shown in Figure 13.4 with Transaction MC40, and it shows
an ABC analysis based on consumption/usage values 1. You access the second screen
with Transaction MC41, and it shows an ABC analysis based on requirements values 2.
Figure 13.4 ABC Analysis Using Transactions MC40 and MC41

13 Material Requirements Planning
678
Let’s now discuss both types of material replenishment procedures—deterministic
and stochastic (or consumption-based)—in greater detail, along with an overview of
the MRP types associated with them:
 Deterministic replenishment procedures and MRP types (type A materials)
You use deterministic planning to plan for the future; that is, the MRP run will create
order proposals in a quantity and for a time period exactly as required, without tak-
ing any elements of probability or randomness into account.
For example, a material that is planned using MRP type PD (see Section 13.2.1) will
always create a proposal for the exact quantity to meet the material demand and at
the time it’s needed. This MRP type usage is most prevalent due to its easy setup
because it doesn’t involve any automation. To add some quantity buffer to this plan-
ning type to ensure enough stock will be available during the replenishment lead
time, as well as to prevent stock-out, you maintain a safety stock. But because the
planning of each individual material demands equal and individual consideration of
various other factors, such as whether the material’s demand is high or low and
whether the lead time to replenish is long or short, this MRP type isn’t effective
when used across large numbers and diverse types of materials.
MRP type PD is planning deterministically. (SAP calls this MRP.) PD simply looks at
demand in the future and covers it with a procurement proposal that has a delivery
date just before the demand occurs. Other deterministic MRP types are P1, P2, P3, P4,
and D1. They require you as a planner to set a planning time fence, and they help you
by keeping planned orders that are generated by the MRP run outside that period of
time. These MRP types can also protect a short-term production program and are
fixed automatically when they fall within the frozen zone. You use MRP type M0 to
plan important finished (or unfinished) products separately from the rest. It has its
own master production scheduling planning run and is meant to cut down on pro-
cessing time before the plan is released to the BOM structure. As in the P series, the
M1, M2, M3, and M4 types allow for the protection of a planning time fence and auto-
matic fixing of procurement proposals. MRP types D1 and D2 (which are based on
demand-driven replenishment) work the same as MRP type M1, with the difference
that stocks are replenished to the maximum level.
 Consumption-based replenishment procedures and MRP types (type B and type C
materials)
These procedures are also called stochastic and have some degree of randomness
and an element of probability. The future planning of their demand and correspond-
ingly the supply availability of these materials is based on past consumption pat-
terns. Hence, there is a probability and randomness to this process that can be buff-
ered with safety stocks, capacity, or time.
In stochastic planning procedures, the system tries to cover future requirements by
making stock levels available that fulfill these future requirements with the least
amount of inventory investment. That is why consumption-based replenishment
© 

679

sections.title: Deterministic Replenishment Procedures
sections.text:
13.2 Deterministic Replenishment Procedures
policies underlie an estimating process. Here, the safety stock settings for these pro-
cedures play an equally important role. Because future stockholdings are predicted
to the best of the procedure’s capabilities and because there is some level of random-
ness and variability in demand, a buffer in the form of safety stock in most cases is
indispensable to avoid stock-outs.
The need for a buffer level depends on the length of the replenishment lead time, the
variability of that lead time, the variability in demand, and a desired, stipulated ser-
vice level that communicates the planner’s percentage of acceptable stock-outs to the
system. Using these parameters, the system—or for manual procedures, the plan-
ner—can calculate a safety buffer that should deliver the expected results in terms of
availability and stockholding. The first groupings of consumption-based replenish-
ment procedures are the reorder point types. Reorder-level planning is a consump-
tion-based method because it requires inventory to be available at all times and
doesn’t wait until there is demand before triggering replenishment. The next group-
ing is forecast-based planning procedures, such as MRP type VV. Finally, the third
grouping is time-phased planning procedures, such as MRP types R1 and R2.
In the next section, let’s discuss the deterministic replenishment procedures and the
associated MRP types available in the SAP S/4HANA system. The section after that will
cover stochastic replenishment procedures and their associated MRP types.
13.2 Deterministic Replenishment Procedures
When you as a planner decide to plan a material using MRP, one of the first parameters
to define in the material master is the MRP type. An MRP type is a control function you
use to control several subsequent steps. For example, if you assign the MRP type ND,
for no planning, to a material, then the system won’t plan the material at all. If you use
MRP types that start with P (e.g., PD, P1, P2), they control how the system takes the net
requirements calculations into account and how it firms procurement proposals for
MRP during MRP runs. The net requirements calculation takes the plant stock and all
receipts into account and then subtracts the requirement quantities. If the net require-
ments calculation comes up with a shortage or deficit quantities, then the system cre-
ates procurement proposals during the MRP run.
In the net requirements calculation, the system determines the available stock based
on the following formula:
Plant stock – Safety stock + Open order quantity (purchase orders, production orders,
fixed procurement proposals) – Requirement quantity (PIRs, customer requirements,
material reservations, forecast requirements for unplanned additional requirements)
= Available stock
If it evaluates that there is no shortage, then the system doesn’t create procurement
proposals.

13 Material Requirements Planning
680
Note
We’ll cover only the standard materials replenishment procedures and their associated
MRP types, to help you develop the necessary foundations and understanding required
to use them before opting for customization.

sections.title: Material Requirements Planning Type PD
sections.text:
13.2.1 Material Requirements Planning Type PD
MRP type PD is planning deterministically. In its initial configuration, PD may also
include a forecast of unplanned consumption (see the Forecast Ind. and Cons. Ind. Fore-
cast fields). When a forecast model is maintained in the Forecasting screen, the PD will
forecast unplanned consumption (the unplanned consumption can be seen in the con-
sumption history) and display additional requirements (Unpld. Cons.) in the stock
requirements list (via Transaction MD04).
PD simply looks at demand in the future and covers it with a procurement proposal
that has a delivery date just before the demand occurs, as shown in Figure 13.5.
Figure 13.5 MRP Type: PD
Because PD is deterministic, it waits for demand so as to arrange supply. If there is
demand, and if the MRP run is executed, then a supply proposal is generated to cover
that demand. This MRP type is well suited for expensive purchased parts that are costly
to store, for which consumption is highly variable and unpredictable, and for which the
lead time to procure them is short.
MRP run generates

13.2 Deterministic Replenishment Procedures
This type of planning is carried out for the quantities planned in PIRs or quantities
planned in incoming sales orders (MTO or MTS), dependent requirements, and stock
transfer requirements—that is, any requirement that is MRP relevant. In this type of
net requirements calculation and its planning, the following apply:
 The system doesn’t consider or ask for any forecast information or historical con-
sumption values. Material planning is ensured only on the basis of absolute require-
ments or demands in hand.
 The system determines the available stock in the planning run, which is required to
meet the demands, with the following logic:
Plant stock + Scheduled receipts from production and purchase – All of the demands
(e.g., from sales orders, material reservations, and PIRs)
If the available stock is insufficient to fulfill the demand, then depending on the set-
tings of the planning run, PD creates planned orders for in-house production, as well as
purchase requisitions, delivery schedules and scheduling lines, or purchase requisi-
tions for external procurement.
As mentioned earlier, other deterministic MRP types are P1, P2, P3, and P4, which
require you as a planner to set a planning time fence. They help you by keeping
planned orders that are generated by the MRP run out of that period of time. The differ-
ences among these four MRP types lie in whether they firm or don’t firm the procure-
ment proposals and what requirements in which period they consider.
Similarly, you use MRP type M0 to plan important finished (or unfinished) products
separately from the rest. As in the P series, the M1, M2, M3, and M4 types allow for the
protection of a planning time fence and automatic fixing of procurement proposals.
Let’s consider an example of firming type P1 to explain how firming works. In firming
type P1, the system automatically firms the existing procurement proposals so long as
these are within the defined planning time fence. It also creates new order proposals
when it evaluates that there is going to be a shortage of material within the planning
time fence. However, the system pushes the new procurement proposals outside the
planning time fence to ensure that the production plan isn’t disturbed. When the newly
created procurement proposals begin to fall within the planning time fence, the system
automatically firms them and makes them a part of the production plan.
To ensure greater stability in the production and procurement plans, a planner can
take advantage of firming logic and the planning time fence. The firming logic defines
how the system should handle the existing or new procurement proposals by automat-
ically firming the planned orders, allowing an option for manual firming, or doing nei-
ther of those things. The planning time fence allows for the definition of a range within
which MRP doesn’t create, change, move, or delete any procurement proposals. This
can be a great reliability factor for the production department if you use a planning

13 Material Requirements Planning
682
time fence such as the following: for the next five days starting from today, the produc-
tion plan will not be changed, not even if there are new sales orders.
You can maintain the planning time fence in the MRP 1 view of the material master or
in configuration. The planning time fence is the number of workdays (not calendar
days) within which a planner can protect the master plan. The system firms the pro-
curement proposals to protect them from any changes. However, a planner still has the
flexibility to make any last-minute manual changes, if needed. Firming of the procure-
ment proposals (planned orders, purchase requisitions, and delivery schedules) falling
within the planning time fence prevents the proposals from being adapted in the next
planning run. In other words, inside the planning time fence, the system doesn’t create
or delete any procurement proposals, nor does it change the existing proposals.
The system calculates the planning time fence from the current date and adds up the
number of working days incorporated as the planning time fence in the material master
(see the MRP 1 view). You can also define the planning time fence in the configuration
settings of the MRP group because it’s easier to just assign the MRP group to the mate-
rial master. When the system moves forward in the time scale, it’s logical that any pro-
curement proposal that was previously outside this planning time fence will start to
move into the planning time fence. When that happens, the proposal is automatically
firmed for protection from changes. That means the horizon keeps rolling with time.
There are two firming options for procurement proposals: automatic firming and man-
ual firming.
There are ten MRP types with firming available in the system (M0, M1, M2, M3, M4, P1,
P2, P3, P4, and D1), and they differ with regard to the automatic firming of elements
within the planning time fence and also with regard to the creation of new elements.
It’s important to note that firming type M0 behaves just like MRP type PD; that is, the
planning time fence is inactive for this master production scheduling time.
Note
Firming type M refers to master production scheduling, whereas firming type P refers
to MRP.
Technically, master production scheduling and MRP in SAP S/4HANA are almost the
same thing, and you can use the same transaction for them (MD01N). However,
because neither master production scheduling nor MRP takes capacity restrictions into
account, we recommend that you split the planning run between master production
scheduling and MRP and run capacity leveling for the products that you’ve planned
with master production scheduling before running MRP.
© 

683
13.2 Deterministic Replenishment Procedures
Table 13.1 shows the similarities among firming types for both MRP (firming type P) and
master production scheduling (firming type M).
A planner can also set how the system should account for roll-forward. A roll-forward
defines the number of workdays starting from today (both forward and backward)
during which the system can delete the firmed planned orders if they aren’t converted
to either production orders or purchase requisitions. For example, you may want to
delete all of the firmed planned orders older than five days during the next MRP run. To
do this, you select an MRP type with firming (e.g., P1) and select the Delete Firm Planned
Orders option from the Roll Forward field. Next, define “–5” in the Roll Forward field in
the MRP group parameters for a specific plant (using Transaction OPPR). The negative
value (the number of days from today) is for deletion of the past and firmed planned
order, and a positive value is to manage deletion of the firmed planned orders in the
future. In the same MRP group settings (in Transaction OPPR), you assign the number
of workdays (from today) that the system can use to firm the planned orders for the rel-
evant firming type.

sections.title: Material Requirements Planning Type VB
sections.text:
13.2.2 Material Requirements Planning Type VB
VB is the simplest and most manual of the reorder point MRP types. When using MRP
type VB, you as a planner have to calculate and set the reorder point manually and
maintain it in the Reorder Point field in the material master. You can base the reorder
point on historical consumption values or even your best guess. Although it’s not
required, we recommend that you to maintain safety stock when using this MRP type.
Furthermore, we suggest that you to look at the past consumption of the material in
question and derive an estimated, future daily consumption quantity from that
amount. Then, simply multiply that quantity by the number of working days (the days
on which your company consumes the material) within the total replenishment lead
time. You should maintain that lead time in the MRP 2 screen of the material master
record (in the Planned Delivery Time + GR Proc.Time + Purchasing Time fields). The
product of future daily consumption with the lead time gives an inventory point that,
if reached, will enable the system to reorder so that your company doesn’t run out of
stock before the next receipt comes in. Depending on the variability in the estimated
consumption or lead time, you may need to raise the reorder point by a safety stock
when the reorder point shifts below the safety stock (see Figure 13.7).
© 

685
13.2 Deterministic Replenishment Procedures
Figure 13.7 Reorder Point Planning
Tips and Tricks
There are many ways to calculate and guess future daily consumption. Transaction
MC.9, for example, gives that history. However, when you’re looking at the table with
key figures and characteristics, you’ll see that only monthly totals are available, and
even though you can divide the monthly total by the number of working days in that
month, you’ll likely have some sense of doubt about consistent consumption day by
day. But the consumption graphic is displayed in the LIS, where you can apply a number
of helpful tools to get all the information you need. This way, you can see whether
there’s consistency in consumption over time and figure out how much inventory your
company needs to get through the lead time without any stock-outs. Then, you can
add a safety stock and maintain it in the material master’s MRP 2 view. Subsequently,
you can maintain MRP Type VB and also Reorder Point in the MRP 1 view of the mate-
rial master. The reorder point quantity is the estimated consumption within the total
replenishment lead time plus the safety stock quantity.
We’ve mentioned that even though you’re not required to maintain a safety stock at all
in MRP 2, you should have that kind of information readily available (see Figure 13.8).
The system generates exception message 96 (Section 13.13.3 on exception messages)
every time the stock falls into that range, which gives an indication over time of
whether the reorder point is too low or there’s an opportunity to lower it (and with it,
the average stockholding of this material). The more often exception message 96
appears, the more likely it is that the reorder point is too low. But if exception message
96 appears only rarely, it’s time to look into lowering that reorder point.
We’ll show you an example of reorder point planning in Section 13.15.2.

sections.title: Material Requirements Planning Type VM
sections.text:
13.2.3 Material Requirements Planning Type VM
To achieve more automation and greater accuracy in the reorder point procedure, you
can use MRP type VM, which eliminates guesswork and lets the system do the figuring.
But the system can only figure and calculate reliable and authentic values if the under-
lying basic data and specifically the historical consumption figures are accurate. This
procedure requires timely and accurate GR/GI postings because it uses that data to fig-
ure mean absolute deviation and therefore consistency in consumption, lead time
deviation, and various settings (e.g., planned delivery time, service level) to come up
with a reorder point. If any of this information isn’t maintained correctly, VM will pro-
duce reorder points that either cause stock-outs or drive the inventory to potentially
very high levels. In any case, VM uses the material master’s Forecasting screen to auto-
matically calculate the reorder point and a safety stock quantity via the following pro-
cedure:
1. On the Forecasting screen, VM uses its settings and historical consumption to calcu-
late a past MAD. The system does so by comparing an ex-post forecast to the actual
past consumption.
2. It uses the service level being maintained in the MRP 2 view of the material master
and a standard formula to calculate a safety stock quantity.
3. It calculates the future anticipated daily consumption by running a forecast in the
material master record.
4. It multiplies the working days in the total replenishment lead time with the future
anticipated daily consumption. This is the basic value that will appear in the fore-
casting results.
5. It adds the safety stock quantity to the basic value. This is the calculated reorder
point.

13 Material Requirements Planning
688
6. During this procedure, VM uses the Forecasting screen for simulation purposes only.
The forecasted quantities won’t become MRP relevant. VM uses the forecast to sim-
ply perform an ex-post forecast that allows for the determination of MAD, which it
then uses to determine the degree of variability for the calculation of reorder point
and safety stock (see Figure 13.10).
Figure 13.10 MRP Type VM
When deciding whether to use this planning procedure, you should take the following
factors into consideration:
 The higher the degree of variation (MAD), the higher the safety stock requirement
and the higher the reorder point will be.
 The longer the replenishment lead time, the higher the reorder point needs to be to
get through the replenishment lead time.
 The longer the replenishment lead time, the greater the chance of unforeseen con-
sumption. This increases variability and, with it, safety stock requirements and the
reorder point.
 The higher the service level percentage maintained in MRP 2, the higher the safety
stock requirement and, with it, the reorder point.
 The higher the daily consumption, the higher the reorder point. At the same time,
the forecast is obligatory and uses total consumption.

and other calculations.

sections.title: Material Requirements Planning Type V1
sections.text:
13.2.4 Material Requirements Planning Type V1
MRP type V1 acts exactly like VB, except that it takes external requirements into con-
sideration. You as a planner have to manually set and calculate the safety stock and
reorder point this procedure. You can choose and configure what the external require-
ments are, such as order reservations, released stock transport orders, and released
purchase requisitions. If there is a sales order reservation within the replenishment
lead time (set Ext. Reqmts as “2”), then the order is triggered early because the amount
of demand from the reservation is taken into account for the determination of when
the reorder point is broken.
While you’re using this MRP type, it’s helpful to use a lot-size procedure that adds those
external requirements to the ordering quantity within the lead time. Because these
demands are already known, it makes sense to add them to the order size so that at the
time of receipt, the consumption can be replenished in full. Therefore, the V1 type
offers an advantage over both the VB type and the VM type in that it’s not purely driven
by the past but is a little more proactive and includes future requirements in the plan-
ning process.

sections.title: Material Requirements Planning Type V2
sections.text:
13.2.5 Material Requirements Planning Type V2
MRP type V2 provides the most sophisticated and advanced reorder point procedure so
far. Not only does it include external requirements, but it also calculates the safety

13 Material Requirements Planning
690
stock and a reorder point automatically by using the Forecasting screen in the material
master record. As a word of caution, beware of using too much automation too soon,
unless you fully understand this material planning procedure.
As shown in Figure 13.12, MRP type V2 automatically calculates a reorder point based on
length of lead time, variability in the consumption, and the service level you set. So, the
V2 type combines automatic calculation of the planning parameters with consider-
ation of external demand. This procedure is based on a simple reorder point methodol-
ogy that is sophisticated enough to automate the planning process and optimize
inventory holdings.
Figure 13.12 MRP Type V2

sections.title: Material Requirements Planning Types D1 and D2
sections.text:
13.2.6 Material Requirements Planning Types D1 and D2
In demand-driven replenishment (DDR), the planning run creates a new supply element
(such as a purchase requisition, a planned order, or a stock transfer requisition) when
the net flow position of a material falls below the reorder point maintained in the
material master. The aim of the planning run is to create supply elements to fulfill
demand by using a better quality of demand signal. This means that it’s possible to cre-
ate supply elements that meet real customer demand more accurately, which in turn
achieves improved service levels on the one hand while lowering inventory levels and
bound capital on the other.
As this MRP type uses firming type 1 (like MRP type M1), the procurement proposals
within the planning time fence are firmed automatically as soon as their date is at least
one day before the end date of the planning time fence. The date of new procurement
proposals created in the planning time fence is then moved to the end date of the plan-
ning time fence. As a result, these new procurement proposals aren’t firmed.

sections.title: Stochastic Replenishment Procedures
sections.text:
13.3 Stochastic Replenishment Procedures
The equation for generating supply elements is the net flow equation:
On-hand stock + Open supply – Open past (including today’s) demand – Qualified spike
demand
In this equation, the quantity of stock on hand is added to the quantity of open supply,
and then then the total quantity of open demand (including today’s demands) plus the
qualified spike demand is subtracted. This results in the net flow position. If the day’s
net flow position is below the reorder point, a supply element is created for the amount
that will bring the stock level back up to the maximum stock quantity.
SAP S/4HANA 2023 has introduced a new MRP type, D2, to cater to the DDR require-
ments of the repetitive manufacturing industry. (Refer to Chapter 8 on repetitive man-
ufacturing.)
Note
See Chapter 14 for more information on DDR.
13.3 Stochastic Replenishment Procedures
Some of the stochastic replenishment or consumption-based MRP procedures require
you to execute and save the forecast before the system carries out MRP. In such cases,
the Forecasting indicator in the relevant MRP type is marked as mandatory/obligatory,
rather than optional. When a forecast on a material is executed, the system looks for
the forecasting parameters that are maintained in the forecasting view of the material
master, such as the forecasting model and historical or forecast (future) periods. Fur-
thermore, depending on the model you select, it also considers smoothing factors,
such as alpha, beta, gamma, or delta. In forecasting, the system considers the planned
and unplanned consumption quantities of the material for the periods under evalua-
tion and comes up with forecast (future) figures. When the forecast results are saved,
the MRP for forecast-based consumption planning uses these forecast figures to create
procurement proposals after the MRP run.
The following stochastic replenishment procedures require you to execute a forecast
as a prerequisite to an MRP run:
 Forecast-based planning
 Time-phased materials planning
In the following sections, we cover forecast-based and time-phased materials planning.

13 Material Requirements Planning
692

sections.title: Material Requirements Planning Type VV
sections.text:
13.3.1 Material Requirements Planning Type VV
The system updates the consumption data in the material master as total consumption
(planned consumption and unplanned consumption) or unplanned consumption. To
view the consumption details of the material, you go to the Basic Data view of a mate-
rial master (Transaction MM02) and choose the Additional Data icon. Of the many tabs
available, the Consumption tab holds consumption quantities. Per the MRP reserva-
tions, the system updates the consumption data of the planned consumption. If con-
sumption isn’t planned per MRP reservations, or if the material issuance quantity is
greater than the reservation quantities, then the system updates the unplanned con-
sumption.
In MRP type configuration, you choose the type of historical consumption of a material
to plan whether the system uses total consumption or unplanned consumption. Also,
choose the reduction method for forecast requirements. The default system setting is
reduction by consumption.
Since we’ve already covered forecasting in detail in Chapter 11, Section 11.4, we’ll only
briefly cover the details that are relevant to forecast-based consumption planning here.
The consumption forecast for components is based on material withdrawals. You
should maintain the forecast parameters in the Forecasting view of the material mas-
ter, as shown in Figure 13.13.
Figure 13.13 Forecasting View of Material Master
The parameters include the granularity of the forecast—that is, whether it’s carried out
on the basis of monthly, weekly, or daily values. You can define the granularity by using
© 

693
13.3 Stochastic Replenishment Procedures
the Period Indicator field. Other entries specify the number of historical periods based
on which the forecast is carried out, as well as the number of forecast periods. You can
either specify the forecast model directly or have it determined automatically. Some of
the possible forecast models include the following:
 Constant model (D)
 Trend model (T)
 Seasonal model (S)
 Seasonal trend model (X)
 Moving average (G)
Clicking the Execute Forecast button, which is located further down the screen shown
in Figure 13.13 (but not shown in it), enables you to carry out a forecast in the material
master, while clicking the other two buttons, Consumption vals and Forecast values
(also not shown), allows you to edit the historical values and the forecast values, respec-
tively.
You can also run an individual forecast for a material or an overall forecast for several
materials. To run an individual material forecast, use Transaction MP30. After execut-
ing the forecast, you can change the forecast results, and you can also correct the fore-
cast values interactively by using Transaction MP31. To display the forecast, use Trans-
action MP32.
For the overall forecast, use Transaction MP38 to restrict the planning scope by plants,
materials, and ABC indicators. You can maintain the ABC indicator in the MRP 1 view of
the material master.
In MRP type VV, you carry out the forecast at an individual material level. In fact, each
material can have its own forecasting screen activated in the material master. You can
also use MRP type VV for finished goods, and there are many situations that call for set-
ting up a finished product as VV. MRP type VV is a consumption-based replenishment
strategy in that it maintains inventory in anticipation of actual demand. The inventory
is replenished to a forecast, which is based on the material’s own consumption history—
hence, the name material forecast. This is a good strategy when demand is predictable
but the lead time to replenish is long. By putting “artificial” demand out there by using
the forecasting functionality, MRP can generate all supply elements far ahead of time.
All that you need to do is convert the requisition into an order on the date that the sys-
tem suggests (see Figure 13.14).
Warning
VV doesn’t take demand spikes into consideration. Any changes in demand will flow
into the consumption pattern and eventually be picked up by the forecast module. The
system might increase or decrease the forecast or state that the current underlying
model isn’t applicable anymore. So, as with all the other strategies, you should use
MRP type VV if and when it meets your business needs.

13 Material Requirements Planning
694
Figure 13.14 MRP Type VV
For MRP type VV to function, you must maintain all the parameters in the Forecasting
screen of the SAP S/4HANA system. You can identify the periodicity by which to run
the forecast to create demand with the Period Indicator in the Forecasting view of the
material master. This setting will eventually determine with what frequency the fore-
cast is run automatically for the material in question. The options are listed here:
 Daily (D)
This option results in daily demand records for every working day being visible in
Transaction MD04 and relevant to the MRP run. Be aware, however, that a daily fore-
cast will also result in daily replenishment, which may be an unnecessary consump-
tion of the system’s resources.
 Weekly (W)
This is the most often used periodicity, and it allows for relatively low average inven-
tory holding by bringing in less inventory than in a monthly periodicity. It’s also
flexible and quick to detect spikes and periods of low consumption.
 Monthly (M)
This is used for less important or less expensive items because it brings in large
quantities with few orders.
 Posting period (P)
You can freely define this according to a period that you set up in financial account-
ing. After you set the Period Indicator, you should decide how many periods from
the past to include in the forecast calculation. You can also set aside a number of
periods for an ex-post forecast, and you can identify the number of periods for
which the forecast run generates demand.

sections.title: Material Requirements Planning Types R1 and R2
sections.text:
13.3.2 Material Requirements Planning Types R1 and R2
Materials that are replenished using a time-phased planning method are scheduled so
that the MRP run considers them for planning only on specific dates in the week. This
is determined by the planning cycle in which the MRP run is only triggered on specific
days of the week. Time-phased planning can be carried out with consumption-based—
stochastic—procedures or with deterministic ones. MRP type R1 simply plans for an
MRP run on the days scheduled by the planning cycle, which might lead to stock-outs
when large consumption happens on a day not included in the planning cycle. This is

13 Material Requirements Planning
696
where MRP type R2 is the better choice because it calculates a reorder point automati-
cally and triggers the planning run whenever the reorder point is broken.
For example, if a supplier always delivers a material on a certain day of the week due to
its own internal route and delivery planning, then it makes sense for a company’s plan-
ning run to follow the same cycle and be displaced by the delivery time. Similarly, if the
company procures or produces certain material during specific times of the year only,
then the company should use time-phased planning. For example, chocolate manufac-
turers in a few hot and humid climatic countries cease production during the summer
months. In such cases, all production and procurement planning is confined to the
defined planning cycle only.
To plan a material based on time phases, you use Transaction MM02 to enter the MRP
type for time-phased planning and the planning cycle in the material master (see
Figure 13.17). You also use a forecast in this planning concept, and the general procedure
is very similar to forecast-based planning, with the difference that the procurement
intervals are predefined.
Figure 13.17 Time-Phased Materials Planning
The configuration basics of setting up a planning cycle are the same as those for setting
up a planning calendar, which is described in Section 13.14.

sections.title: Lot Sizing Procedures
sections.text:
13.5 Lot Sizing Procedures
13.5 Lot Sizing Procedures
After performing a net requirements calculation, when the system determines a mate-
rial shortage, it looks to information maintained in the Lot Sizing Procedure field in the
MRP 1 view of the material master. The lot sizing specifies the quantity that the system
should propose when it comes across a quantity shortage of a material. When the sys-
tem creates the correct lot size as a procurement proposal, it also looks for other infor-
mation, such as the safety stock, maximum or minimum lot size, static or dynamic
safety stock, scrap percentage, and rounding value.
It’s very important to determine lot sizes to ensure optimum inventories and good ser-
vice levels. Suppliers occasionally demand minimum order quantities, which end up as
excess inventory levels, especially for slow-moving items. It’s possible to maintain
inventory controls or gates that are then easier to manage. Between procurement or
purchasing and materials planning, there is a gate, which means that materials plan-
ning should generate the most economical lot size, generate an order proposal with
that optimal lot size, and initiate the purchasing process.
The advantage of having these kinds of gates is that a company can build a basis for
negotiations with suppliers. For example, if the materials planner applies the condi-
tions in the Minimum Lot Size field in the material master, then the MRP run will
always generate order proposals using the minimum quantity. Lot sizing procedures in
the SAP S/4HANA system help the planner find the most optimal quantities to order.
An MRP run not only automatically generates the order proposal but also suggests the
perfect lot size to bring in so that availability is assured without the company holding
too much inventory. There are several lot sizing procedures already preconfigured and
available in the standard SAP system.

You assign the lot sizing procedure in the MRP 1 view of the material master (Transac-

(Lot-for-lot order quantity).
Figure 13.20 Lot Sizing Procedure in Material Master
There are three different lot sizing procedures in the SAP S/4HANA system:
 Static lot sizing procedures
 Periodic lot sizing procedures
 Optimum lot sizing procedures
We’ll discuss these procedures in the following sections before moving on to configur-
ing lot sizes.

sections.title: Static Lot Sizing Procedures
sections.text:
13.5.1 Static Lot Sizing Procedures
Static lot sizing procedures can further be categorized as exact lot sizing, fixed lot siz-
ing, fixed lot sizing with splitting, and replenish to maximum stock level, as described
in the following sections.
Lot Size: Exact Lot Sizing
The system creates planned orders or procurement proposals to cover the exact short-
age requirement only (EX). For example, if a net requirement quantity of a material is
© 

701
13.5 Lot Sizing Procedures
110 units, then the system creates the procurement proposal for 110 units only (see
Figure 13.21).
Figure 13.21 Lot Size: EX
Lot Size: Fixed Lot Sizing
The system proposes the total requirement in multiples of fixed lots (FX). For example,
if the lot size is fixed as 60 units, and the shortage quantity is 240, then the system cre-
ates four planned orders to cover the shortages.
Lot Size: Fixed Lot Sizing with Splitting
The system proposes the total requirement in multiples of fixed lots with overlap (FS).
This lot sizing is generally preferred if a company can’t procure a large quantity at once
and hence must split the quantity with an overlap. Using this lot sizing procedure
entails assigning a fixed lot quantity together with a rounding value. The rounding
value must be a multiple of the fixed lot quantity. For example, if the lot size is fixed as
100 units, and if the rounding value is 20 units, then the system creates five planned
orders to cover the shortages (see Figure 13.22).
Figure 13.22 Lot Size: FS

You can also shift the orders by a certain takt time, which means using a control param-
eter for scheduling based on takts (i.e., physical areas of a production line). This proce-
dure avoids the simultaneous scheduling of numerous orders and thus ensures that
the actually required lead time is observed. If you use splitting in combination with a
fixed lot size, then the rounding value must be a multiple integer of the fixed lot size.
The takt time indicates the offset between order dates in terms of workdays. The differ-
ent orders overlap in time and are shifted against each other by the takt time.
For example, you can assign Lot Sizing Procedure as “FS” (Fixed Lot with Splitting) and
give a Fixed Lot Size of “100.” Next, set the Rounding Value as “25” (it should be a multi-
ple of the fixed lot quantity). Finally, maintain a Takt Time of “3” days (workdays). When
the system runs the MRP, assuming there is no existing stock of the material in the
plant, it will create four planned orders of 25 each for the total quantity of 100 and will
also delay the start date of each planned order by three workdays (takt time).
Lot Size: Replenish to Maximum Stock Level
The system creates planned orders to fill to the maximum stock level (HB). The maxi-
mum stock level has to be defined in the relevant field of the MRP 1 view of the material
master. For example, if the maximum stock level is 300 units, and the demand quantity
is 50 units, then the system creates a planned order of 350. It first ensures that the 300
units of stock fill up, and then, it adds the demand quantity to it.
When the reorder point is broken, the MRP run figures the difference between the cur-
rent stock level and the maximum stock level desired and creates a replenishment
quantity correspondingly.
When you’re using this lot sizing procedure, some consumption occurs during the
replenishment lead time, and when the replenishment quantity is received, the stock
level might be considerably lower than at the time you calculated the replenishment
quantity. One way to address this issue is to include demand in the replenishment lead
time by making relevant configuring settings in a lot sizing procedure, as covered in
Section 13.5.4.

sections.title: Periodic Lot Sizing Procedures
sections.text:
13.5.2 Periodic Lot Sizing Procedures
Periodic lot sizing procedures can be further categorized into daily lot sizing, weekly lot
sizing, monthly lot sizing, and lot sizing of any other periodicity. For example, if the lot
sizing procedure is daily, then the system considers demand with all the receipts for
that day and creates a daily shortage proposal in case a shortage exists. If it’s a weekly
lot size, then the system combines all the requirements for a week and comes up with
one procurement proposal.
In other words, several demands can be covered with a single supply element. These
procedures all collect demand in a specified period and create exactly one supply
© 

703
13.5 Lot Sizing Procedures
element to cover the total demand quantities in that period. However, it’s important to
mention that the longer the period, the more inventories need to be maintained. This
is because the MRP run generates an order proposal that brings in the entire quantity
needed in that period to the date of the first demand. For example, if a planner is using
a three-month period, and if there’s a demand of one piece on the first date of the first
month, then the order proposal would bring in the entire quantity needed for three
months’ worth of demand on the first day. This would result in higher inventory, which
would carry a cost if the item in question is of high value. Periodic lot size procedures
are effective when used with cheap items with low inventory carrying costs.
Tips and Tricks
Use Transaction OMI4 to set the availability date as either the start or the end of the
period (see Section 13.5.4).

sections.title: Optimum Lot Sizing Procedures
sections.text:
13.5.3 Optimum Lot Sizing Procedures
In the optimum lot sizing procedure, the production or purchase of materials entails
fixed lot size costs (such as setup costs for machines and purchase order costs) and vari-
able costs (which are referred to as capital tie-up due to stockholding). To minimize
variable costs, you must procure quantities that are as small as possible—but to mini-
mize fixed costs, you must procure quantities that are as large as possible. Therefore,
optimum lot sizing procedures consider variable and fixed costs and determine opti-
mal lot sizes according to different procedures based on these costs.
Example
Phosphoric acid is the main component used in the production of diammonium phos-
phate, which is a specialized fertilizer. The company wants to replenish to the maxi-
mum stock of phosphoric acid at the time of placing new orders, but there are supplier
requirements regarding minimum and maximum order limits for shipment. To cater to
this business need, you start by creating a new MRP lot size (which may also be a copy
of MRP lot size HB: replenish to maximum stock level) and checking the Check Min. Lot
Size and Check Max Lot Size boxes. You then assign this self-defined MRP lot size to the
MRP 1 view of the material master. The system will then prompt you to enter the mini-
mum and maximum lot sizes of the material. Thereafter, not only will the system make
sure to replenish the material to the maximum stock level, but it will also take the min-
imum and maximum lot sizes into account when creating procurement proposals.
To illustrate this example further, say that the storage tank capacity for phosphoric
acid is 40,000 metric tons, the minimum lot size is 20,000, and the maximum is
25,000. Currently, the stock in the storage tank has dropped to 10,000 metric tons. The
system creates one planned order for 25,000, which ensures that this is the maximum

13 Material Requirements Planning
704
lot size it can go up to and still remain within the maximum storage tank capacity of
40,000. If the minimum and maximum lot sizes had not been maintained, then the
system would have created a planned order of 30,000 metric tons to replenish the
existing stock of 10,000 to the maximum of 40,000.
Optimum lot sizing procedures strive to strike a balance between ordering cost and
stockholding cost, and they therefore optimize the total cost of inventory. For exam-
ple, let’s look at the EX lot sizing procedure, which brings in—in its simplest version—
exactly as much as is needed at exactly the time that it’s needed. In other words, there’s
no need to carry inventory forward to meet any other demand; whatever comes in is
consumed immediately. However, there’s certainly an ordering cost that needs to be
paid every time there is a demand. So, if there are five demands in one month, then a
company will end up paying for five orders. This is different with a monthly lot sizing
procedure, in which there’s only one order per month. But if you as a planner consider
the same situation with five demands, then the order will bring in the total quantity to
meet all demands throughout the months to fulfill the first demand, and you will have
to carry forward the stock that isn’t consumed by the first demand. On the other hand,
there’s only the cost of one order.
But if you’re using an optimum lot sizing procedure instead, then the system (the MRP
run) will figure out automatically—in any given demand situation—what the opti-
mum order quantity and order frequency are to arrive at the minimum between the
ordering cost and stockholding cost. The system does so by using various formulas,
such as the least unit cost formula, the Groff formula, or the part-period balancing for-
mula. These all aim to strike the optimum balance between ordering and stockholding
costs.
To determine the variable costs, you maintain the storage costs percentage specified in
the storage costs indicator (Storage Costs Ind.). You enter the storage costs indicator
along with the ordering costs in the MRP 1 view of the material master, and the system
will calculate the variable costs according to the following formula:
Variable costs = Requirement × Price × Storage costs [%] × Storage duration ÷ 365
In the MRP 1 view of the material master, you maintain the Storage Costs Ind. as well as
the Ordering Costs.

Although standard lot sizes available in the SAP S/4HANA system are generally suffi-

an exact lot size calculation.
Figure 13.23 Lot Size Configuration
You can also set different applicable lot sizing parameters for short-term and long-term

term horizon, while the long-term horizon enables the forecast function, which is
mostly aggregated (e.g., via monthly periodic lot sizing). This is often useful in reducing

are smaller than the average requirement quantity. Typically, a period-based lot size is
used for the long-term horizon.
Tips and Tricks
If you use fixed lot sizes for financial or practical reasons, without any technical need,
then you can avoid surplus procurements by checking the Last Lot Exact box in the

sections.title: Rounding
sections.text:
13.6 Rounding
configuration of the MRP lot size. This is particularly applicable to use with higher-
value goods.
13.6 Rounding
In some cases, it’s useful to round the quantities of the procurement proposals. In the
simplest business scenario, you can use a rounding value because the order quantity of
the requirement coverage element always represents a multiple of the rounding value.
In more complex scenarios, in which the rounding value depends on the requirement
quantity, you can use a rounding profile, which enables you to, for example, model dif-
ferent pack sizes.
The system offers static and dynamic rounding profiles. The static rounding profiles
round up the value after a threshold value has been reached. If the rounding value
exceeds the threshold, the system continuously rounds up the value until it reaches
the next threshold. You can create rounding profiles by using Transaction OWD1.
Example
If the rounding value is 30 units, the procurement quantity is 75 units, and the mini-
mum lot size is 15 units, then the system always rounds up the planned order to a
multiple of 30 units, specifying the rounding to a quantity of 30 units. In this example,
the system rounds up the procurement quantity of 75 units to 90. You can use the
Rounding Value option in the MRP 1 view of the material master in almost all lot sizing
procedures.
The static rounding value method may not fulfill all business scenarios in which you’re
using a given rounding value and the procurement proposals are rounding to that value.
There may also be business processes in which the rounding value may change based on
the size of the procurement proposal. In such instances, you can use a static rounding
profile. For example, you can configure the system to have a rounded value of 10 units if
the procurement quantity is at least one unit. For a procurement quantity of 31 units or
more, the system should round it off to 50 units.
Using Transaction OWD1, the example in Figure 13.24 1 shows that a Rounding Profile
PP01 has been created for Plant 1710. Here, you choose the Static button, and the next
screen 2 will pop up. Enter the threshold values and the rounding values and then click
the Simulation button to bring up the Simulation Selection dialog box 3. Enter a Quan-
tity simulation of “1.000” unit, enter a to qty of “150.000,” and click Continue. The sys-
tem will then display the simulation results 4.
If there’s a business need to specify how the system should act when it reaches a certain
threshold value by either adding up or reducing the quantity to the defined percentage,

13 Material Requirements Planning
708
you can do that too. Go back to the first screen shown in Figure 13.24 1, and this time,
choose the Create qty t/b added/subtracted button. The screen shown in Figure 13.25
will appear, and there, you can define the threshold values and then enter the percent-
age that the system should add to or subtract from the procurement quantity. So, if the
procurement quantity is less than or equal to 100 PC, the system will add up to 10% of
it. When the procurement quantity is greater than 100 but less than or equal to 200, it
will reduce the quantity by 15%.
Figure 13.24 Rounding Profile (Static)
Figure 13.25 Quantity Addition/Subtraction Profiles
In a dynamic rounding profile, you define the rounding off method as well as the
rounding rule. The rounding off method may be a multiple of the order unit or sales
order. Also, you should consider using an available customer exit to define any unique
© 

709

sections.title: Scrap
sections.text:
13.7 Scrap
rounding method. For a rounding rule, you round the threshold value either up or
down by the defined percentage.
13.7 Scrap
Companies are often faced with problems relating to materials they can’t use, which are
known as scrap. They can come from defective finished goods produced, excess con-
sumption of raw material due to wastage in the production process, and operational
inefficiencies. The system offers several options for maintaining scrap or waste details
at each level—the assembly level and the component (raw material) level—so that pro-
duction and procurement planning are closely synchronized with actual and practical
situations.
Based on historical data or practical experience, you should maintain assembly and
component scrap as master data, which subsequently facilitates production and pro-
curement processes. Scrap is treated differently in the SAP S/4HANA system. Scrap in
real life can be available for use or even sold, but it isn’t treated as inventory in the SAP
S/4HANA system.
The two types of scrap described here are as follows:
 Assembly scrap
This is completely unusable finished or semifinished product for which the raw
material was issued in accordance with a BOM. Assembly scrap is different from
coproducts or by-products, which are inventory managed in the SAP S/4HANA sys-
tem.
 Component scrap
This allows the system to increase the issuance quantity of the component against a
production order by the defined percentage to account for scrap or wastage during
production.
Successfully assigning assembly and component scrap in master data enables the sys-
tem to consider scrap not only during production planning—either by increasing pro-
duction quantity to account for assembly scrap or increasing the issuance quantity to
account for component scrap—but also during procurement planning. During the
MRP run, the system takes the scrap percentage into account and reflects it in the plan-
ning proposals it generates.
To maintain the details of assembly or component scrap in the MRP view of the mate-
rial master, you follow menu path Logistics  Materials Management  Material Master 
Material  Change  Immediately or use Transaction MM02.
In the following sections, we’ll provide additional details about the different types of
scrap and also explain how to maintain details of their different uses.

13 Material Requirements Planning
710

sections.title: Assembly Scrap
sections.text:
13.7.1 Assembly Scrap
You maintain assembly scrap in the MRP 1 view of the material master. Here, you enter
the assembly scrap in the form of a percentage in the Assembly Scrap (%) field. If this
material is then used as an assembly, the system considers it during scrap calculation
by correspondingly increasing the production order quantity. For example, say an
assembly scrap of 10% is defined for the material. When a production order is created,
say for a quantity of 100 PC, the system automatically increases the production order
quantity to 110 PC to account for 10% scrap. At the same time, it also issues an informa-
tion message. Because the production order quantity has increased to 110 PC, the sys-
tem accordingly increases the components’ quantities as well, as it reads this informa-
tion from the material’s BOM (and multiplies the production order quantity, which in
this case is now 110 PC with the components’ quantities).

sections.title: Component Scrap
sections.text:
13.7.2 Component Scrap
You maintain component scrap in the MRP 4 view of the material master in the form of
a percentage. If this material is then used as a component in the BOM, the system con-
siders it during scrap calculation. For example, entering 10% scrap for 100 M of a com-
ponent material increases the component issuance quantity in the production order to
110 M (see Figure 13.26).
Figure 13.26 Component Scrap

sections.title: Operations and Component Scrap in Bill of Materials
sections.text:
13.7.3 Operations and Component Scrap in Bill of Materials
In Transaction CS02, to change a BOM for a material, you choose the Item Overview col-
umn. On the screen that appears (see Figure 13.27), you maintain operation scrap and
component scrap in percentages. When maintaining operation scrap as a percentage,
you also check the Net ID box.
© 

711
13.7 Scrap
Figure 13.27 Operation and Component Scrap in BOM
If both operation scrap and component scrap are maintained in the BOM, then the sys-
tem takes both of them into account. For example, for the component quantity of 1.00
M, the system calculates the final component issuance quantity as 1.21 M, after first tak-
ing operation scrap and then component scrap into account. That is, it makes the calcu-
lation 1.00 M × 1.10 = 1.10 M and then makes the calculation 1.10 M × 1.10 = 1.21 M.
Tips and Tricks
If you maintain a component scrap percentage in a material master (as in Figure 13.26),
it will enable the system to consider the same scrap percentage whenever and wher-
ever this component is used in any material’s BOM. However, if there’s a business sce-
nario in which you maintain different scrap percentages of the component used in dif-
ferent products, then it makes sense to maintain the component scrap details in each
material’s BOM (as in Figure 13.27).
For example, raw tomatoes are used as a component in producing different products. If
the same quantity of raw tomatoes is wasted (as scrap) in producing different prod-
ucts, then you should maintain this scrap percentage in the material master for fresh
or raw tomatoes. However, if raw tomato wastage percentages are different for differ-
ent products, such as tomato ketchup, tomato paste, tomato puree, and tomato soup,
then you should maintain these individual wastage (scrap) percentages in each of the
four tomato-based products so that the SAP system correctly will calculate the raw
tomato quantities required to procure and/or produce each of the four products and
their associated wastages.

sections.title: Scrap in Routing
sections.text:
13.7.4 Scrap in Routing
You use Transaction CA02 to change a routing for a material. In the Item Overview col-
umn, go to the General Data area (see Figure 13.28), where you can maintain operation
scrap as a percentage. Then, when a production order is created, the system will
increase the component issuance quantity by the percentage you define to account for
operation scrap in the routing.

13 Material Requirements Planning
712
Figure 13.28 Scrap in Routing

sections.title: Safety Stock
sections.text:
13.8 Safety Stock
Safety stocks are quantity or time buffers that companies use to account for variability
in supply and demand. The system offers two types of safety stock: static (i.e., absolute)
safety stock and dynamic safety stock (i.e., safety days’ supply).
In the following sections, we’ll cover how safety stock availability can be used in net
requirements calculation and also the selection method that the system uses for
receipts.

sections.title: Static Safety Stock
sections.text:
13.8.1 Static Safety Stock
The system can subtract static or absolute safety stock from material availability calcu-
lations (net requirements calculations). The safety stock must always be available to
cover for unforeseen material shortages or unexpected high demand (see Figure 13.29).
Figure 13.29 Static Safety Stock
Receipts
Forecasted demand
Safety stock
30
80
120
Actual demand
Dead stock
© 

713
13.8 Safety Stock
Static safety stock certainly has its uses, especially when the objective is to guarantee
100% availability of a cheap part. However, in most situations, the system will drive up
dead stock because variability in demand may lead to constant availability of stock that
may never really be used.
The simplest buffering strategy of all is to use a static safety stock that you can main-
tain manually in the Safety Stock field (see Figure 13.30). If you maintain a safety stock
quantity, the MRP run will ignore that quantity for planning purposes and set it aside
for a rainy day. In fact, the Stock/Requirements List area in Transaction MD04 subtracts
the safety stock on the first line and then works without it for everything it does and
looks at.
Figure 13.30 Safety Stock and Safety Days’ Supply
Figure 13.31 (Transaction MD04) shows that the safety stock quantity is immediately
subtracted from free available stock and will simply be ignored in all future planning.
Figure 13.31 Not Considering Safety Stock for the Available Quantity

sections.title: Safety Stock Availability
sections.text:
13.8.2 Safety Stock Availability
To avoid the unnecessary creation of planned orders for a smaller quantity, which can
otherwise be covered by the safety stock, you can define the percentage (share) of safety
stock that the system can use in such business scenarios. For example, during the MRP

13 Material Requirements Planning
714
run, say the system assesses a shortage of 2 PC of a material. However, a safety stock of
40 PC is maintained for this material, which the system doesn’t consider in the MRP run.
If a 10% safety stock has been defined to account for smaller shortages—which, for this
example, means that 4 PC come from safety stock—then the system can cater to this
small requirement or shortage of 2 PC from the 4 PC available from safety stock and will
no longer create a procurement proposal.
To define the safety stock parameters, you use menu path SAP IMG  Logistics  Produc-
tion  Material Requirements Planning  Planning  MRP Calculation  Stocks  Define
Safety Stock Availability. For this example, the system can consider 10% of the safety
stock for Plnt 1710 and MRP Group 0000 for net requirements calculation (see Figure
13.32). Next, you assign this MRP group in the MRP 1 view of the material master.
Figure 13.32 Share of Safety Stock

sections.title: Dynamic Safety Stock with Range of Coverage Profile
sections.text:
13.8.3 Dynamic Safety Stock with Range of Coverage Profile
You can employ a dynamic safety stock in the SAP S/4HANA system by using the Range
of Coverage profile. With the receipts-forecasted demand safety stock profile, you can
set a target dynamic safety stock, a minimum level, and a maximum level. If effectively
implemented, the MRP run will always try to keep the target dynamic safety stock
available for planning. But, other than with the static safety stock, no additional quan-
tities will be brought in unless the safety stock falls below the minimum. Therefore, if
the minimum low in planning is set, then all safety stock is used as a buffer. In addition,
if the actual demand turns out to be less than what was planned, the maximum level in
the coverage profile will keep the stock from blowing up in case of underconsumption
of the planned demand.
If there is a fixed lot size or a minimum order quantity, then the target coverage may
be exceeded. However, the coverage profile also has a maximum coverage due to a
fixed lot size or a rounding value.
In a coverage profile, you don’t set the levels (minimum, target, and maximum) in
quantities, but you manage them in days of coverage. That fact provides the dynamic
part of the safety stock because the objective is to hold three days of future demand in
reserve to buffer demand and supply variability. That means that if demand goes up,
there’s more safety stock, and if demand goes down, there will be less.
© 

715
13.8 Safety Stock
To set up the range of coverage profile, you follow menu path SAP IMG  Production 
Material Requirements Planning  Planning  MRP Calculation  Define Range of Cover-
age Profile (Dynamic Safety Stock) or use Transaction OMIA (see Figure 13.33).
Figure 13.33 Range of Coverage Profile
You use the Determine Range of Coverage area to calculate the future daily demand.
You do this by collecting demand over a horizon (you have to set the horizon here in
the first section) and then dividing the total by the number of days within that horizon
(working days or calendar days must be identified in this section) to arrive at a future
average daily demand. You then multiply this future average daily demand by the lev-
els set in the Determine Average Requirements area. Therefore, at the end of this calcu-
lation, you as a planner will end up with quantities for the minimum target and the
maximum target. For example, if the levels are Minimum = 1, Target = 5, and Maximum
= 20, and if the future average daily demand is 10 pieces for the next three months,
then the levels will be 10 pieces minimum, 50 pieces for the target dynamic safety
stock, and 200 pieces for the maximum desired stock level.
It’s important to note that these numbers are only valid for any period when the future
average daily demand is 10 pieces. If that changes, the levels will change also. Figure
13.33 shows the Coverage Profile set to “001”.
The dynamic safety stock is calculated with this formula:
Dynamic safety stock = Average daily requirement × Range of coverage

13 Material Requirements Planning
716
In this equation, the average daily requirement is calculated with this formula:
Requirements in the specified periods ÷ Number of days in the total period length
For the calculation of the average daily requirements, you must maintain the following
parameters:
 Period Indicator
This indicator determines the calendar period for which average daily requirements
need to be carried out. You can maintain the following values: weeks (W), months
(M), and periods according to the planning calendar (K). For this example, you main-
tain “M” for a monthly coverage of safety stock.
 Type of Per.Length
You use this indicator to determine the length of the individual periods in detail.
You can maintain the following values: workdays, calendar days, and standard days.
If you select standard days as a period length, then you must maintain the number
of days determined per period in the field.
 Number of Periods
You define and use the number of periods to calculate the average daily require-
ments.
 Range of Coverage Profile in the First Period
The Number of Periods is three months. The No. of Days per Period is twenty days.
 Range of Coverage in the Second Period
The target safety stock (Tgt) is enough to cover five days.
 Range of Coverage in the Rest of the Horizon
You must plan a minimum stock of “1” day, a target stock of “6” months, and a max-
imum target safety stock of “8” days.
Figure 13.34 shows the MRP 2 view of the material master, wherein the previously con-
figured Range-of-Coverage Profile 001 for plant 1710 is assigned.
Figure 13.34 Assigning Coverage Profile in Material Master
With these configuration and master data settings in place, let’s now demonstrate the
dynamic safety stock calculation with an example.
Figure 13.35 shows the monthly requirement (PIRs, via Transaction MD61) shown in the
Planned qty column for the next five months for Material 2267 and Plant 1710.
© 

717
13.8 Safety Stock
After you run MRP Live (in Transaction MD01N), you go to the stock/requirements list
(in Transaction MD04).
Let’s evaluate the planning results in detail. Because it’s already July, the system calcu-
lates safety stock requirements for the next month (August) by considering the average
daily requirement for the next three months (August, September, and October). Here,
the system considers three months because in the coverage profile, the period main-
tained was three months.
Figure 13.35 Independent Requirements
Note
All requirements and calculations are in PC (pieces), and all calculations are rounded
off to two decimal places.
Because the target days of the first period in the coverage profile is maintained as three
days of daily average requirements (6.16 PC), the amount to cover this requirement of
three days of safety stock (dynamically calculated) is calculated as follows:
6.16 (average daily requirement) × 3 (three days of target safety stock) = 18.51 PC of
safety stock (period 1)
Figure 13.36 shows that in August 2024, the system created a planned order for a quan-
tity of 120 PC.
Figure 13.37 shows the summarized view, in which the Targ. (target) Stock column vali-
dates the safety stock calculations shown earlier.

13 Material Requirements Planning
718
Figure 13.36 Procurement Proposals Created after MRP Run
Figure 13.37 Actual Coverage and Days’ Supply

sections.title: Safety Stock and Safety Time
sections.text:
13.8.4 Safety Stock and Safety Time
For days’ supply and safety time, the system plans the GR in advance by the period
specified as the safety time. Thus, the planned days’ supply of the stock actually corre-
sponds to the number of days specified as the safety time. The system shifts back the
date of the receipts by the number of working days and also takes the factory calendar
into account.
In the MRP 2 view of the material master, the Safety Time/Act.Cov. field is available. The
system uses the number of days maintained in this field to simulate the requirements
of an earlier date, displaced exactly by that number of days. By using this field, a plan-
ner pulls the requirements in so that the MRP run creates the receipts for replenish-
ment order proposals for an earlier date. Therefore, the safety time is a time buffer and
© 

719
13.8 Safety Stock
doesn’t increase any quantities. Because the actual requirement doesn’t change, the
only difference is that the receipt comes in earlier and provides additional coverage for
the time it was pulled forward.
The fact that the safety time brings the receipts in earlier, when they’re actually needed,
means that when there’s a supply delay, the safety time provides a time float and thus
works alongside the safety stock, which acts as a quantity float.
You make the relevant entries in the MRP 2 view of the material master. The system then
refers to the planning of safety days’ supply as the safety time. To consider the safety
time, you set the Safety Time Ind. to “1” (for independent requirements) or “2” (for all
requirements) and then also give the Safety Time/Act.Cov. in days. Furthermore, you
